perturbation.original_text: 5. Except as otherwise pr ovided in any future agreem ent, at any time, Company agrees that it shall return to the AOC upon request all documents, records, notebooks, computer media or other stored informati on of any form or type whatsoever containing any Confidentia l Information, including all copies thereof, then in its possession or contro l (directly or indirectly), whether prepared by it or others and it shall at such time immediately discontinue all
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Except as otherwise pr ovided in any future agreem ent, at any time, Company agrees that it shall return to the AOC upon request all documents, records, notebooks, computer media or other stored informati on of any form or type whatsoever containing any Confidentia l Information, including all copies thereof, then in its possession or contro l (directly or indirectly), whether prepared by it or others; however, the company will have 180 days to complete this action and will continue all
perturbation.explanation: By changing the return of confidential information from 'upon request' to '180 days' creates conflict with the need to protect sensitive government data efficiently. 180 days is far too long for data of this nature.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 1798.82(a) mandates that any agency that owns or licenses personal information of a California resident shall implement and maintain reasonable security procedures and practices appropriate to the nature of the information, to protect the personal information from unauthorized access, destruction, use, modification, or disclosure. The modification introduces a contradiction since delaying the return of confidential information for 180 days significantly increases the risk of unauthorized access or disclosure, violating the requirement for reasonable security measures.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81. CUSTOMER RECORDS [1798.80 - 1798.84] ( Title 1.81 added by Stats. 2000, Ch. 1039, Sec. 1. ) 1798.82. (a) A person or business that conducts business in California, and that owns or licenses computerized data that includes personal information, shall disclose a breach of the security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach in the security of the data to a resident of California (1) whose unencrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person, or, (2) whose encrypted personal information was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the encryption key or security credential was, or is reasonably believed to have been, acquired by an unauthorized person and the person or business that owns or licenses the encrypted information has a reasonable belief that the encryption key or security credential could render that personal information readable or usable. The disclosure shall be made in the most expedient time possible and without
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction because the original text requires the company to return confidential information 'upon request,' while the changed text allows them 180 days to complete this action. This delay directly conflicts with the legal obligation to maintain reasonable security procedures and practices to protect personal information from unauthorized access or disclosure, as described in the Law Explanation referencing California Civil Code § 1798.82(a). Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating that a business shall disclose a breach of security of the system following discovery or notification of the breach 'in the most expedient time possible.' The 180-day delay does not align with 'the most expedient time possible' to secure data, creating a substantial risk of unauthorized access and thus a legal contradiction. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where time-sensitive obligations in a contract do not align with legal requirements.

perturbation.original_text: 9. The parties acknowledge and agree t hat the Confidential Information and rights related thereto being protected by the AOC her eunder are of a special, unique, unusual and extr aordinary character, which gives them a peculiar value, the loss of which may not be a dequately or reasonably compensated for in damages in an action at law, and further agree that the breach by Company of any of the pr ovisions of this Agreem ent shall cause the other party irreparable injury and damage. In such event, the party alleging breach of this Agreement shall be entitled, as a matter of right, without further notice, to require of the other party specific performance of all of the acts and undertakings required of the other party hereunder and to obtain injunctive and other equitable relief in any competent court to prevent the violation or threatened violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement by the other party. Neither this provision nor any exer cise by either party of its rights to equitable relief or specific performance herein granted shall constitute a waiver by either party of any other rights which it may have to, damages or otherwise. If either party brings su it to enforce the terms hereof, the successful party in such suit shall be ent itled to receive all of its reasonable costs of litigation, including attorneys' fees.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. The parties acknowledge and agree t hat the Confidential Information and rights related thereto being protected by the AOC her eunder are of a special, unique, unusual and extr aordinary character, which gives them a peculiar value, the loss of which may not be a dequately or reasonably compensated for in damages in an action at law, and further agree that the breach by Company of any of the pr ovisions of this Agreem ent shall cause the other party irreparable injury and damage. In such event, the party alleging breach of this Agreement shall be entitled, as a matter of right, with further notice of 90 days, to require of the other party specific performance of all of the acts and undertakings required of the other party hereunder and to obtain injunctive and other equitable relief in any competent court to prevent the violation or threatened violation of any of the provisions of this Agreement by the other party. Neither this provision nor any exer cise by either party of its rights to equitable relief or specific performance herein granted shall constitute a waiver by either party of any other rights which it may have to, damages or otherwise. If either party brings su it to enforce the terms hereof, the successful party in such suit shall be ent itled to receive all of its reasonable costs of litigation, including attorneys' fees.
perturbation.explanation: By adding a 90-day notice requirement before seeking specific performance, the modification conflicts with the immediate relief typically available for breaches of confidentiality agreements, potentially causing further damage during the waiting period.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 526 outlines situations where an injunction may be granted. Specifically, it allows for injunctive relief when it appears during the litigation that a party is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. This section does not impose a mandatory 90-day notice period before seeking injunctive relief. The modification introduces a contradiction, because waiting 90 days would make this relief potentially ineffectual.

OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 7. OTHER PROVISIONAL REMEDIES IN CIVIL ACTIONS [501 - 574] ( Heading of Title 7 added by Stats. 1974, Ch. 1516. ) CHAPTER 3. Injunction [525 - 534] ( Chapter 3 enacted 1872. ) 526. (a) An injunction may be granted in the following cases: (1) When it appears by the complaint that the plaintiff is entitled to the relief demanded, and the relief, or any part thereof, consists in restraining the commission or continuance of the act complained of, either for a limited period or perpetually. (2) When it appears by the complaint or affidavits that the commission or continuance of some act during the litigation would produce waste, or great or irreparable injury, to a party to the action. (3) When it appears, during the litigation, that a party to the action is doing, or threatens, or is about to do, or is procuring or suffering to be done, some act in violation of the rights of another party to the action respecting the subject of the action, and tending to render the judgment ineffectual. (4) When pecuniary compensation would not afford adequate relief. (5) Where it would be extremely difficult to ascertain the amount of compensation which would afford adequate relief. (6) Where the
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a 90-day notice period before seeking specific performance, which contradicts the immediate injunctive relief typically available for breaches of confidentiality agreements under California Code of Civil Procedure § 526. According to snippet 1, section 526 (a), injunction may be granted when it appears during litigation a party threatens to do an act violating the rights of another party. The added delay of 90 days directly undermines the purpose of injunctive relief, which is to prevent immediate or ongoing harm, as the confidential information could be further disseminated during the waiting period, thus rendering the judgment ineffectual.

perturbation.original_text: 13. This Agreement and all rights and oblig ations hereunder shall inure to and be binding upon the parties hereto and thei r respective successors, affiliates, agents, employees and assigns . Neither party may assi gn any of its rights or obligations hereunder without t he prior written consent of the other party. This Agreement shall be gover ned by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Califor nia without regard to choice of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: 13. This Agreement and all rights and oblig ations hereunder shall inure to and be binding upon the parties hereto and thei r respective successors, affiliates, agents, employees and assigns . Either party may assi gn any of its rights or obligations hereunder without t he prior written consent of the other party. This Agreement shall be gover ned by, and construed and enforced in accordance with, the laws of the State of Nevada without regard to choice of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from California to Nevada could potentially create conflict depending on how the laws of the two states differ regarding trade secrets and confidential information. Although both states have laws regarding these issues, the nuances in legal interpretation and available remedies may vary. Nevada is more lenient regarding certain business practices and employee mobility. In contrast, California is known for its strong protection of trade secrets and strict enforcement of non-compete agreements.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA), codified in Cal. Civ. Code § 3426 et seq., provides legal remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. By changing the governing law to Nevada, the contract may no longer be subject to CUTSA's protections, potentially weakening the remedies available to the AOC in case of trade secret misappropriation. This creates a contradiction, because the original agreement was drafted under the assumption that California law would apply, including the specific protections offered by CUTSA.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies California law, which includes the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The changed text replaces California law with Nevada law. The Law Explanation indicates that this shift might weaken trade secret protections for the AOC, as CUTSA provides specific legal remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 confirms the existence of CUTSA as codified in California law (CIV 3426 et seq.). The perturbation explanation indicates that Nevada may be more lenient in business practices and employee mobility. Therefore, by changing the governing law, the contract contradicts the original intent of adhering to the potentially stronger trade secret protections afforded by California law, specifically CUTSA.

perturbation.original_text: 14. This Agreement shall survive until such time as all Confid ential Information disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of Company. Except as otherwise provided in any future agreement, either party may terminate this Agreement by written notice to the other. Not withstanding any such termination, all rights and obligations hereunder shall survive with respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 14. This Agreement shall survive for a period of one (1) year from the effective date, regardless of whether the Confid ential Information disclosed hereunder becomes publicly known and made generally available through no action or inaction of Company. Except as otherwise provided in any future agreement, either party may terminate this Agreement by written notice to the other. Not withstanding any such termination, all rights and obligations hereunder shall not survive with respect to Confidential Information disclosed prior to such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Limiting the agreement's survival to one year, regardless of whether the information becomes public through no fault of the company, severely weakens the protection of confidential information, potentially conflicting with the reasonable measures required to safeguard sensitive data.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 3426.1 specifies the remedies available for misappropriation of trade secrets, including injunctive relief to prevent further misuse. By limiting the agreement's survival to only one year and nullifying the surviving rights and obligations, the ability to obtain injunctive relief to protect those trade secrets is compromised, especially if misappropriation occurs after the one-year period. This contradicts the law's intent to provide ongoing protection for trade secrets as long as they remain confidential and valuable.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The changed text limits the agreement's survival to one year, regardless of the confidential information becoming public through no fault of the company. This directly contradicts the intent of California Civil Code § 3426.1, which, as supported by the snippet from CIV 3426.1, aims to protect trade secrets from misappropriation, especially through 'breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. By limiting the survival and nullifying rights after one year, the ability to seek injunctive relief to protect trade secrets is compromised, especially if misappropriation occurs after that one-year period.
